states.name: New York
states.framework: NYDFS BitLicense / Limited Purpose Trust Charter
states.detail: Most coins commonly called 'stablecoins' count as Virtual Currency under New York's rules, which means issuing one requires a BitLicense or a New York-chartered trust company (the trust route also lets a company handle money transmission and fiduciary duties without extra licenses, which a BitLicense alone doesn't allow). Before issuing, a coin also needs DFS sign-off through self-certification, a spot on DFS's approved 'Greenlist,' or individual approval. Coins already on the Greenlist include Gemini Dollar, Ripple USD, and WisdomTree Dollar.
states.sourceText: NY Dept. of Financial Services, Virtual Currency Businesses

states.name: California
states.detail: California's Digital Financial Assets Law entered into force on July 1, 2026. Any exchange, custodian, stablecoin issuer, or Bitcoin ATM operator serving California residents must now hold a DFPI license or have filed a complete application, or face civil enforcement of up to $100,000 per day. The law bars licensees from exchanging a stablecoin unless its issuer is licensed, has applied for a license, or is a qualified bank or trust company backing the coin with eligible securities. DFPI expects an initial $100,000 net worth, subject to change, and began accepting applications through NMLS on March 9, 2026.
states.effective: In force since Jul. 1, 2026; DFPI license or complete application now required; enforcement active

states.name: Texas
states.framework: Money Services Modernization Act (Finance Code Ch. 152)
states.detail: Texas treats qualifying stablecoins as 'money,' which means issuing or transmitting them requires a money transmission license. To count, a stablecoin must be pegged to a currency like the dollar, fully backed by reserve assets, and redeemable for that currency on demand. Licensees must hold a minimum net worth that scales with their size, starting at $100,000. Texas also lets a licensee hold stablecoin itself as part of its required reserves, as long as it's the same stablecoin it owes to customers and is kept in proper custody.
states.sourceText: Tex. Finance Code Ch. 152, enacted by S.B. 895 (2023)

states.name: Florida
states.detail: Florida enacted a package of digital-asset laws signed by Governor DeSantis in late June 2026, all effective immediately. HB 175 requires a money services business to hold a license from the Office of Financial Regulation before issuing a payment stablecoin, with reserves backed one-to-one by cash or similarly safe assets and verified monthly by a CPA, aligning the state framework with the federal GENIUS Act. SB 1568 establishes the Florida Stablecoin Pilot Program, allowing the Department of Financial Services to accept approved payment stablecoins for certain government fees. A companion law, HB 505, requires virtual-currency kiosk operators to register with the OFR.
states.effective: HB 175 and SB 1568 signed by Gov. DeSantis, late Jun. 2026; effective immediately
states.sourceText: Florida House/Senate, HB 175 and SB 1568 bill history

states.name: Wyoming
states.detail: The only state to both regulate and directly issue a stablecoin. The Wyoming Stable Token Commission's Frontier Stable Token (FRNT) can only be backed by cash, US Treasuries maturing within a year, or short-term Treasury repurchase agreements, per W.S. 40-31-106(a). A Trust Account must hold 100% of FRNT's notional value, plus a separate Liquidity Fund Account targeting another 2% as a buffer, with leftover investment earnings flowing quarterly to a state school foundation program. FRNT runs across eight blockchains and went live for public purchase on January 7, 2026, redeemable for $1.00.
states.effective: Mainnet live since Aug. 18, 2025; public purchase since Jan. 7, 2026

states.name: Illinois
states.framework: Digital Assets and Consumer Protection Act (DACPA) + Digital Asset Kiosk Act (DAKA)
states.detail: Signed the same day, August 18, 2025 (DACPA enacted as Public Act 104-0428), these twin laws split digital asset oversight in two. DACPA defines a 'digital asset' broadly enough to deliberately capture stablecoins: an asset normally excluded for not being used as an investment loses that exclusion if it's marketed as holding a stable, effectively fixed value, which is exactly how a stablecoin is sold. Businesses must register with IDFPR by July 1, 2027, though customer disclosure and custody protections apply sooner. DAKA separately regulates digital asset kiosks specifically, capping daily transactions at $2,500 for new customers and $10,500 for existing ones, and limiting fees to the greater of $5 or 18% of the transaction.
states.effective: Both effective Aug. 18, 2025; DACPA registration deadline Jul. 1, 2027
states.sourceText: 205 ILCS 731/ and 205 ILCS 732/, Illinois Compiled Statutes

states.name: Colorado
states.detail: Colorado repealed its old Money Transmitters Act and replaced it with the CSBS Model Money Transmission Modernization Act, enabling multistate licensing participation. Unlike Texas, Colorado writes no virtual currency or stablecoin language into the statute at all: 'monetary value' is defined simply as 'a medium of exchange, whether or not redeemable in money,' leaving it to interpretation whether a given stablecoin activity falls within that generic definition. Its net worth formula is identical to Texas's, since both states adopted the same model law: $100,000 or 3% of assets up to $100 million, then a declining percentage above that.

states.name: Washington
states.detail: Washington's statute defines 'virtual currency' directly and writes it into the definition of 'money transmission' itself, so there's no separate crypto license, just the existing money transmitter framework. Virtual currency licensees face specific disclosure duties: fee schedules, whether funds are insured, a notice that virtual currency transfers are irrevocable, and how errors get resolved. The standard net worth formula (as low as $10,000) jumps to a $100,000 minimum specifically for companies that provide virtual currency storage, though that figure comes from the implementing regulation, not the statute itself.

states.name: Arizona
states.detail: Arizona replaced its old money transmitter statute with the CSBS model act in 2022 (SB 1580, Chapter 236). Like Colorado, the new statute defines 'monetary value' as simply 'a medium of exchange, whether or not redeemable in money,' with no explicit virtual currency or stablecoin definition. Whether a given stablecoin activity triggers licensing depends on how Arizona DIFI interprets that generic definition. Cryptocurrency kiosk operators face separate, explicit requirements under A.R.S. 6-1236, including daily transaction caps of $2,000 for new customers and $10,500 for existing ones, mandatory fraud warnings, and blockchain analytics requirements. Net worth follows the same formula as Colorado and Texas: $100,000 or 3% of total assets up to $100 million.

states.name: Georgia
states.detail: Georgia enacted the Georgia Payment Stablecoin Act (HB 1272) in 2026, a dedicated licensing regime for payment stablecoin issuers, but it is not yet in force. Under Section 4, and subject to appropriations by the General Assembly, it becomes effective on the earlier of January 18, 2027 or 120 days after the issuance of final federal GENIUS Act implementing regulations. The Act carves payment stablecoin issuance out of Georgia's existing money transmission laws and authorizes the Department of Banking and Finance to license issuers, on a framework the statute requires to be substantially similar to the GENIUS Act. Licensed issuers must back outstanding stablecoins one-to-one with liquid reserves held in trust for consumers and maintain redemption policies. Beginning July 18, 2028, it becomes unlawful to offer or sell a payment stablecoin in Georgia unless the issuer is a permitted, licensed, or federally qualified issuer. Separately, some virtual-currency money transmission already requires a license under O.C.G.A. Sec. 7-1-680 et seq.
states.effective: Enacted 2026; effective (subject to state appropriations) the earlier of Jan. 18, 2027 or 120 days after federal GENIUS Act final rules; issuer requirement from Jul. 18, 2028
states.sourceText: HB 1272, Georgia Payment Stablecoin Act (as passed House and Senate)

states.name: North Carolina
states.framework: Money Transmitters Act (N.C. Gen. Stat. Ch. 53, Art. 16B)
states.detail: North Carolina regulates stablecoin and virtual-currency activity through its Money Transmitters Act (Chapter 53, Article 16B), administered by the Office of the Commissioner of Banks (NCCOB). The statute expressly defines virtual currency and brings money transmission conducted in whole or in part in virtual currency, including maintaining control of virtual currency on behalf of others, within its licensing requirement. A person engaging in money transmission must obtain a license through NMLS; the license is perpetual but requires an annual NMLS renewal filing. North Carolina does not yet have a dedicated stablecoin-issuer statute. A separate bill, the Virtual Currency Kiosk Consumer Protection Act (HB 920), would place crypto-ATM operators under NCCOB supervision, with implementing rules due by December 1, 2026.
states.effective: Money Transmitters Act in force; virtual currency covered; kiosk bill (HB 920) pending, rules due Dec. 1, 2026
states.sourceText: N.C. Gen. Stat. Ch. 53, Art. 16B (Money Transmitters Act); NC Office of the Commissioner of Banks

states.name: Connecticut
states.framework: Money Transmission Act (virtual currency revisions, PA 25-66 / HB 7082)
states.detail: Connecticut revised its money transmission law to regulate virtual currency, effective October 1, 2025, administered by the Department of Banking. A person engaged in virtual currency business in Connecticut, by receiving, transmitting, storing, or maintaining custody or control of virtual currency, must hold virtual currency of the same type and amount owed or obligated to each customer. Licensees may not lend, hypothecate, pledge, or otherwise use or encumber customer virtual currency without authorization, and the act extends risk-disclosure and receipt requirements to virtual currency transmitters and kiosk operators. Custody may be delegated only to parties qualified to hold it.
states.sourceText: Conn. Public Act 25-66 (HB 7082); CT Dept. of Banking

states.name: Nevada
states.framework: Money transmission law (NRS Ch. 671), applied to digital currency by FID guidance
states.detail: Nevada has no dedicated virtual-currency or stablecoin statute; its money transmission law (NRS Chapter 671, modernized in 2025) does not expressly mention virtual or digital currency. Instead, the Financial Institutions Division (FID), through published guidance, applies the money-transmission licensing requirement to entities that transmit or hold digital currency, by kiosk, mobile, internet, or other means. Under that guidance, Bitcoin ATM operators must hold a money transmitter license and bond, and licensees holding virtual currency are expected to keep enough of each type to cover outstanding customer obligations. Entities acting purely as digital-asset custodians may instead be regulated as trust companies under NRS Chapter 669. Nevada thus regulates digital assets case by case under existing law rather than a bespoke framework.
states.effective: Money transmission law (NRS Ch. 671) in force; digital currency covered via FID guidance, not express statute
states.sourceText: NRS Ch. 671 (money transmission); applied to digital currency by Nevada Financial Institutions Division guidance
